EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A reinforcing cold front produced very strong and prolonged gusting winds. With several inches of fresh and very blowable snow available, and an additional inch or so of new snow, widespread ground blizzard conditions developed over portions of northeastern ND, mainly from the late forenoon into the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting from 35 to 50 mph produced widespread blowing and drifting snow, with visibilities frequently below 1/4 mile for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a blizzard producing Arctic cold front pushed past the area, the bitterly cold Arctic Airmass dropped into portions of central and eastern ND.  Temperatures overnight and into the morning of the 9th ranged from minus 15 to minus 25 degrees Fahrenheit, and with winds from 15 to 20 mph, wind chills ranged from minus 35 to minus 50 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight low temperatures ranged from minus 15 to minus 25 F, with wind chills from minus 35 to minus 50 F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A reinforcing shot of Arctic air dropped temperatures from minus 15 to minus 35F across all of eastern ND and northwest through westcentral MN, and with winds from 10 to 20 mph, produced wind chills from minus 35 to minus 55 F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight low temperatures dropped from minus 15 to minus 25 F, with winds of 10 to 20 mph producing wind chills from minus 35 to minus 55 F.

A deep low pressure system lifted through the Northern Plains producing heavy precipitation across much of North Dakota. In the west, where colder air worked into the system, much of the precipitation fell as snow. Some of the snow became heavy, particularly over parts of far western North Dakota near the Montana border. The heaviest snow reports of six inches occurred at Williston, Williams County, and Beach, Golden Valley County. So early in the season this had a significant impact on surface transportation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Williston received six inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Colorado Low moved through the area, bringing multiple inches of snow and 30 - 50 MPH winds to the area. This created blowing snow or blizzard conditions in some areas. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Grafton airport reports 44 MPH wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Short-wave upper level troughs lifted into the region resulting in heavy snow developing over southern North Dakota. The snow was the heaviest during the late night hours of February 20 into the early morning hours of February 21. Light snow then continued over many locations during the day into the evening, but northerly winds increased, especially during the overnight hours. This led to blizzard conditions developing over the James River Valley. The most snow was eleven inches in the Fort Yates area of Sioux County, with around ten inches reported in both Adams and McIntosh counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ashley received ten inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow with over 1 inch per hour rates exceeded 6 to 12 inches in total over portions of southeast North Dakota into portions of northwest through west-central Minnesota, heaviest snow near the South Dakota border.  Very storm winds and cold temperatures also produced two separate but concurrent (to the snowfall) periods of blizzard conditions and extremely cold windchills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From 8 to 14 inches of snow was reported across most of the county, along with periods of blizzard conditions as winds frequently gusted to 40 mph. Bitter cold wind chills of from minus 30F to minus 50F through the morning hours of the 21st and 22nd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: From 3 to 5 inches of snow was reported across most of the county, along with periods of significantly reduced visibilities in snow and blowing snow as winds frequently gusted to 40 mph. Bitter cold wind chills of from minus 30F to minus 50F through the morning hours of the 21st and 22nd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions were observed for more than 3 hours at KGAF. Conditions persisted well after the time frame mentioned. I-29 had to be closed from Grafton to the International border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed behind a cold front sweeping through the area, while a short-wave trough passing through resulted in some snow developing. Over the southern James River Valley, the strong winds combined with falling snow and snow on the ground to produce blizzard conditions which lasted from the evening of January 4 until the morning of January 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with falling snow and snow on the ground to produce a blizzard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a rapidly moving arctic cold front, an intensely cold arctic airmass settled into the area. As winds of from 10 to 20 mph persisted into the overnight hours of January 18th into the early morning of the 19th, and temperatures dropped from minus 15 to minus 25F, wind chills dropped from minus 35 to minus 50 F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight low temperatures dropped from minus 15 to minus 25 F, with winds of 10 to 20 mph producing wind chills from minus 35 to minus 50 F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A reinforcing shot of Arctic air dropped temperatures from minus 15 to minus 35F across all of eastern ND and northwest through westcentral MN, and with winds from 10 to 20 mph, produced wind chills from minus 35 to minus 55 F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight low temperatures dropped from minus 15 to minus 25 F, with winds of 10 to 20 mph producing wind chills from minus 35 to minus 55 F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of severe thunderstorms traversed the region starting on the evening of April 22nd. Initially, large hail was the primary concern, with an increasingly tornadic environment heading into April 23rd. Both hail (up to 1.5 inches) and tornadoes were reported with this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report posted via social media. Time estimated based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed behind a cold front sweeping through the area, while a short-wave trough passing through resulted in some snow developing. Over the southern James River Valley, the strong winds combined with falling snow and snow on the ground to produce blizzard conditions which lasted from the evening of January 4 until the morning of January 5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with falling snow and snow on the ground to produce a blizzard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Short-wave upper level troughs lifted into the region resulting in heavy snow developing over southern North Dakota. The snow was the heaviest during the late night hours of February 20 into the early morning hours of February 21. Light snow then continued over many locations during the day into the evening, but northerly winds increased, especially during the overnight hours. This led to blizzard conditions developing over the James River Valley. The most snow was eleven inches in the Fort Yates area of Sioux County, with around ten inches reported in both Adams and McIntosh counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northerly winds combined with fresh snow on the ground to produce a blizzard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta clipper moved through in the evening and late night hours of January 31 into the early morning hours of February 1, with high winds developing in the wake of the cold front. The highest reported wind gust was 74 mph in Ward County, while in Mountrail and Sheridan counties 73 mph winds were registered. Blizzard conditions developed during the early morning of February 1 over parts of north central North Dakota and the James River Valley as the winds combined with falling snow and snow already on the ground. See the January 2022 edition of Storm Data for the start of this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crosby AWOS reported a wind gust of 69 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibilities frequently below 1/4 mile in snow and blowing snow. Winds gusted from 35 to 55 mph as reported at local airports and mesonet stations. Conditions beagn in the early morning and continued through mid-afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving cold frontal system dropped across eastern ND and northwest MN through the afternoon hours of January 18th. Very strong winds speed developed rapidly behind the front, and with abundant fresh and blowable snow, ground blizzard conditions became widespread across the largely exposed terrain of eastern ND and adjacent portions of northwest through westcentral MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting from 40 to 58 mph produced widespread ground blizzard conditions, with visibilities frequently below 1/4 mile.  Abundant fresh and blowable snow led to intermittent whiteout conditions over much of the open countryside.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much of central and eastern North Dakota was impacted by blizzard conditions starting the afternoon of April 11th, and lasting until the evening of April 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations were measured at various points around the Devils Lake Basin, with amounts ranging from 5 to 12 inches. Blowing and drifting snow impacted travel, with visibility of 1/4 mile or less.

The highest winds were 74 mph in Ward County while in Mountrail and Sheridan counties 73 mph winds were registered. The high winds continued into the early morning hours of February 1, 2022, which led to blizzard conditions developing on February 1 over parts of north central North Dakota and the James River Valley as the winds combined with falling snow and snow already on the ground. See the February 2022 edition of Storm Data for a continuation of the high wind event and for the blizzard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A blizzard developed in the afternoon hours on January 4th across eastern ND towards the Red River Valley, and adjacent counties in northwest through westcentral MN. Impacts where felt across the area, with blowing and drifting snow, strong gusting winds, and visibilities at or below 1/4 mile, often at whiteout conditions. This prompted the Travel not Advised across most of eastern ND and within the Red River Valley corridor of MN. Highest wind gusts were observed around 45-52mph across the RRV and towards the west. Snow totals ranged from 3-5 inches around the Highway 2 Corridor, and 1-3 further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted at 35 to 52 mph, with visibilities at or below 1/4 mile due to falling and blowing snow for several hours. From 2 to 4 inches of new snow occurred along with existing blowable snowpack.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta clipper moved through in the evening and overnight hours with high winds developing in the wake of the cold front. The highest winds were 74 mph in Ward County while in Mountrail and Sheridan counties 73 mph winds were registered. The high winds continued into the early morning hours of February 1, 2022, which led to blizzard conditions developing on February 1 over parts of north central North Dakota and the James River Valley as the winds combined with falling snow and snow already on the ground. See the February 2022 edition of Storm Data for a continuation of the high wind event and for the blizzard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Grassy Butte NDDOT site reported a sustained wind of 42 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A blizzard developed in the afternoon hours on January 4th across eastern ND towards the Red River Valley, and adjacent counties in northwest through westcentral MN. Impacts where felt across the area, with blowing and drifting snow, strong gusting winds, and visibilities at or below 1/4 mile, often at whiteout conditions. This prompted the Travel not Advised across most of eastern ND and within the Red River Valley corridor of MN. Highest wind gusts were observed around 45-52mph across the RRV and towards the west. Snow totals ranged from 3-5 inches around the Highway 2 Corridor, and 1-3 further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted at 35 to 52 mph, with visibilities at or below 1/4 mile due to falling and blowing snow for several hours. From 2 to 4 inches of new snow occurred along with existing blowable snowpack.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving cold frontal system dropped across eastern ND and northwest MN through the afternoon hours of January 18th. Very strong winds speed developed rapidly behind the front, and with abundant fresh and blowable snow, ground blizzard conditions became widespread across the largely exposed terrain of eastern ND and adjacent portions of northwest through westcentral MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting from 40 to 58 mph produced widespread ground blizzard conditions, with visibilities frequently below 1/4 mile.  Abundant fresh and blowable snow led to intermittent whiteout conditions over much of the open countryside.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A re-intensifying surface pressure gradient produced winds gusting at 35 to 42 mph and widespread ground blizzard conditions across far southeastern ND and adjacent portions of westcentral MN, through the morning and afternoon of February 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of severe thunderstorms traversed the region starting on the evening of April 22nd. Initially, large hail was the primary concern, with an increasingly tornadic environment heading into April 23rd. Both hail (up to 1.5 inches) and tornadoes were reported with this system.

The highest reported wind gust was 74 mph in Ward County, while in Mountrail and Sheridan counties 73 mph winds were registered. Blizzard conditions developed during the early morning of February 1 over parts of north central North Dakota and the James River Valley as the winds combined with falling snow and snow already on the ground. See the January 2022 edition of Storm Data for the start of this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Logan County had estimated wind gusts of 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 56 mph wind gust was recorded at the Valley City Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much of central and eastern North Dakota was impacted by blizzard conditions starting the afternoon of April 11th, and lasting until the evening of April 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations were measured at various points around the Devils Lake Basin, with amounts ranging from 5 to 12 inches. Blowing and drifting snow impacted travel, with visibility of 1/4 mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta clipper moved through in the evening and overnight hours with high winds developing in the wake of the cold front. The highest winds were 74 mph in Ward County while in Mountrail and Sheridan counties 73 mph winds were registered. The high winds continued into the early morning hours of February 1, 2022, which led to blizzard conditions developing on February 1 over parts of north central North Dakota and the James River Valley as the winds combined with falling snow and snow already on the ground. See the February 2022 edition of Storm Data for a continuation of the high wind event and for the blizzard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A blizzard developed in the afternoon hours on January 4th across eastern ND towards the Red River Valley, and adjacent counties in northwest through westcentral MN. Impacts where felt across the area, with blowing and drifting snow, strong gusting winds, and visibilities at or below 1/4 mile, often at whiteout conditions. This prompted the Travel not Advised across most of eastern ND and within the Red River Valley corridor of MN. Highest wind gusts were observed around 45-52mph across the RRV and towards the west. Snow totals ranged from 3-5 inches around the Highway 2 Corridor, and 1-3 further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted at 35 to 45 mph, with visibilities at or below 1/4 mile due to falling and blowing snow for several hours. From 2 to 4 inches of new snow occurred along with existing blowable snowpack.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A reinforcing cold front produced very strong and prolonged gusting winds. With several inches of fresh and very blowable snow available, and an additional inch or so of new snow, widespread ground blizzard conditions developed over portions of northeastern ND, mainly from the late forenoon into the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting from 35 to 50 mph produced widespread blowing and drifting snow, with visibilities frequently below 1/4 mile for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving cold frontal system dropped across eastern ND and northwest MN through the afternoon hours of January 18th. Very strong winds speed developed rapidly behind the front, and with abundant fresh and blowable snow, ground blizzard conditions became widespread across the largely exposed terrain of eastern ND and adjacent portions of northwest through westcentral MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting from 40 to 58 mph produced widespread ground blizzard conditions, with visibilities frequently below 1/4 mile.  Abundant fresh and blowable snow led to intermittent whiteout conditions over much of the open countryside.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought continued over the western third of North Dakota through the month of February, although there was modest improvement, mainly on the eastern side of the D2 and D3 designated areas. Renville County was able to improve to D1 by February 15th. Refer to the U.S. Drought Monitor for details.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta clipper dropped through the region resulting in strong northwest winds and some light snow over central and parts of Northwest North Dakota. The strongest reported wind gust of 64 mph occurred in Stutsman County. Over Rolette, Pierce, and Foster counties, the strong winds combined with an abundance of snow on the ground to produce a blizzard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minot ASOS reported a sustained wind of 43 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of severe thunderstorms traversed the region starting on the evening of April 22nd. Initially, large hail was the primary concern, with an increasingly tornadic environment heading into April 23rd. Both hail (up to 1.5 inches) and tornadoes were reported with this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated hail size via social media photo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a blizzard producing storm system moved out of the area through the morning of January 5th, an Arctic front and associated Arctic airmass pushed in behind.  The coldest air spread across portions of central and eastern ND for the first evening into the morning of January 6th, and then spread eastward across all of eastern ND and much pf northwest through westcentral MN on the evening of the 6th through the morning of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures plummeted well below zero, ranging from minus 25 to minus 35 degrees Fahrenheit, with wind chills of from minus 35 to minus 50F, throughout the overnight hours of the 5th through the morning of the 6th, and again from the late afternoon of the 6th through the morning of the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A blizzard developed in the afternoon hours on January 4th across eastern ND towards the Red River Valley, and adjacent counties in northwest through westcentral MN. Impacts where felt across the area, with blowing and drifting snow, strong gusting winds, and visibilities at or below 1/4 mile, often at whiteout conditions. This prompted the Travel not Advised across most of eastern ND and within the Red River Valley corridor of MN. Highest wind gusts were observed around 45-52mph across the RRV and towards the west. Snow totals ranged from 3-5 inches around the Highway 2 Corridor, and 1-3 further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted at 35 to 52 mph, with visibilities at or below 1/4 mile due to falling and blowing snow for several hours. From 2 to 4 inches of new snow occurred along with existing blowable snowpack.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a blizzard producing storm system moved out of the area through the morning of January 5th, an Arctic front and associated Arctic airmass pushed in behind.  The coldest air spread across portions of central and eastern ND for the first evening into the morning of January 6th, and then spread eastward across all of eastern ND and much pf northwest through westcentral MN on the evening of the 6th through the morning of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures plummeted well below zero, ranging from minus 25 to minus 35 degrees Fahrenheit, with wind chills of from minus 35 to minus 50F, throughout the overnight hours of the 5th through the morning of the 6th, and again from the late afternoon of the 6th through the morning of the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A reinforcing cold front produced very strong and prolonged gusting winds. With several inches of fresh and very blowable snow available, and an additional inch or so of new snow, widespread ground blizzard conditions developed over portions of northeastern ND, mainly from the late forenoon into the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting from 35 to 50 mph produced widespread blowing and drifting snow, with visibilities frequently below 1/4 mile for several hours.

The highest reported wind gust was 74 mph in Ward County, while in Mountrail and Sheridan counties 73 mph winds were registered. Blizzard conditions developed during the early morning of February 1 over parts of north central North Dakota and the James River Valley as the winds combined with falling snow and snow already on the ground. See the January 2022 edition of Storm Data for the start of this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: McHenry County had estimated wind gusts of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations up to 8 inches impacted the region, followed by gusty northwest winds. Impacts were primarily limited to travel delays due to slick roads and patchy blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread public reports of up to 6 inches of snow were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta clipper moved through in the evening and overnight hours with high winds developing in the wake of the cold front. The highest winds were 74 mph in Ward County while in Mountrail and Sheridan counties 73 mph winds were registered. The high winds continued into the early morning hours of February 1, 2022, which led to blizzard conditions developing on February 1 over parts of north central North Dakota and the James River Valley as the winds combined with falling snow and snow already on the ground. See the February 2022 edition of Storm Data for a continuation of the high wind event and for the blizzard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Carrington AWOS reported a wind gust of 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a blizzard producing storm system moved out of the area through the morning of January 5th, an Arctic front and associated Arctic airmass pushed in behind.  The coldest air spread across portions of central and eastern ND for the first evening into the morning of January 6th, and then spread eastward across all of eastern ND and much pf northwest through westcentral MN on the evening of the 6th through the morning of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures plummeted well below zero, ranging from minus 25 to minus 35 degrees Fahrenheit, with wind chills of from minus 35 to minus 50F, throughout the overnight hours of the 5th through the morning of the 6th, and again from the late afternoon of the 6th through the morning of the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta Clipper type storm system pushed across central and eastern ND, and adjacent portions northwest through westcentral MN, through the overnight and early morning hours of January 13th through the 14th.  Heaviest snow fell along a band from northcentral ND diagonally into southeastern ND, with lesser amounts east of that band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From 5 to 7 inches of new snow were reported by Cooperative Observers, CoCoRaHS Observers, and numerous public reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a rapidly moving arctic cold front, an intensely cold arctic airmass settled into the area. As winds of from 10 to 20 mph persisted into the overnight hours of January 18th into the early morning of the 19th, and temperatures dropped from minus 15 to minus 25F, wind chills dropped from minus 35 to minus 50 F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight low temperatures dropped from minus 15 to minus 25 F, with winds of 10 to 20 mph producing wind chills from minus 35 to minus 50 F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Short-wave upper level troughs lifted into the region resulting in heavy snow developing over southern North Dakota. The snow was the heaviest during the late night hours of February 20 into the early morning hours of February 21. Light snow then continued over many locations during the day into the evening, but northerly winds increased, especially during the overnight hours. This led to blizzard conditions developing over the James River Valley. The most snow was eleven inches in the Fort Yates area of Sioux County, with around ten inches reported in both Adams and McIntosh counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locations northeast of Lemmon, SD received ten inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell across the northern Red River Valley, with strong northerly winds following the snowfall. This lofted snow southward through the valley, leading to blizzard conditions through late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions, driven primarily by light snow and blowing snow, prevailed through the morning. Peak winds reached 45 mph.

Light snow then continued over many locations during the day into the evening, but northerly winds increased, especially during the overnight hours. This led to blizzard conditions developing over the James River Valley. The most snow was eleven inches in the Fort Yates area of Sioux County, with around ten inches reported in both Adams and McIntosh counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ellendale received eight inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Colorado low lifted slowly through the Northern Plains into the Upper Midwest, resulting in a multi-day blizzard over most of North Dakota. Snow spread from southwest to northeast on April 12, with conditions rapidly deteriorating upon the onset of the snow due to the strong easterly winds. By the late afternoon of April 12, Interstate 94 was closed from the Montana state line to Jamestown, with difficult to impossible travel on remaining roads over western and central North Dakota. Winds eventually shifted towards the northwest during the duration of the blizzard as snowfall rates gradually decreased. Still, travel remained impossible as winds continued to gust to around 60 mph over many locations, which combined with the snow on the ground and some falling snow to produce zero visibility. Total snowfall amounts ranged from one to three feet over much of western and central North Dakota, with the highest reports around the Minot area. Multiple one, two, and three day snowfall records were broken. Two direct deaths occurred with this blizzard. One was a 73 year old female in Minot near the Minot Post Office, and the other was a 60 year old male in Watford City along the 200 block of 25th Avenue NE.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing snow and strong winds combined to produce a blizzard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Colorado low lifted through North Dakota. Initially, a wintry mix of rain, freezing rain, sleet and snow developed over western and central North Dakota. As temperatures dropped over the northwest and far north central, ice accumulated on power lines. Strong winds developed, with precipitation eventually changing over to all snow. The strong winds combined with the ice on the power lines leading to thousands of power poles and cross arms breaking, resulting in widespread power outages throughout the northwest into the far north central. On the evening of April 24, almost all of the northwest part of the state was without power. Some residential services in rural areas took over three weeks to have power restored, and some non-residential services were not restored until mid-June. As the snow combined with the strong winds, visibility dropped to zero across western and parts of central North Dakota. Interstate 94 closed from the Montana state line to Mandan, and most other roads were impassable. Due to the after-effects of the blizzard and power outages, the American Red Cross opened warming shelters in Crosby and Williston, and provided showers and meals to many individuals. Snowfall totals of over a foot and a half were reported, though were tough to measure with wind gusts over 70 mph. As of the writing of this episode, power companies and government officials were still in the process of calculating financial losses. With that said, expectations are that at a minimum, the financial impact from this storm was at least 60 million dollars. It should be understood that this value, and the individual county event entries, are rough estimates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and heavy snow combined to produce a blizzard.

Snowfall totals of over a foot and a half were reported, though were tough to measure with wind gusts over 70 mph. As of the writing of this episode, power companies and government officials were still in the process of calculating financial losses. With that said, expectations are that at a minimum, the financial impact from this storm was at least 60 million dollars. It should be understood that this value, and the individual county event entries, are rough estimates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and heavy snow combined to produce a blizzard. In addition, periods of freezing rain occurred which accreted on power lines. This, combined with strong winds, led to extensive damage to power lines, cross arms, and poles, and thus widespread power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms formed during the afternoon, moving across the southern and central portions of the Red River Valley, then into west-central and northwest Minnesota. Wind gusts to 85 mph were reported, along with 2 tornadoes, and numerous other reports of hail and high wind damage.

A strong Colorado low lifted slowly through the Northern Plains into the Upper Midwest, resulting in a multi-day blizzard over most of North Dakota. Snow spread from southwest to northeast on April 12, with conditions rapidly deteriorating upon the onset of the snow due to the strong easterly winds. By the late afternoon of April 12, Interstate 94 was closed from the Montana state line to Jamestown, with difficult to impossible travel on remaining roads over western and central North Dakota. Winds eventually shifted towards the northwest during the duration of the blizzard as snowfall rates gradually decreased. Still, travel remained impossible as winds continued to gust to around 60 mph over many locations, which combined with the snow on the ground and some falling snow to produce zero visibility. Total snowfall amounts ranged from one to three feet over much of western and central North Dakota, with the highest reports around the Minot area. Multiple one, two, and three day snowfall records were broken. Two direct deaths occurred with this blizzard. One was a 73 year old female in Minot near the Minot Post Office, and the other was a 60 year old male in Watford City along the 200 block of 25th Avenue NE.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing snow and strong winds combined to produce a blizzard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Colorado low lifted through North Dakota. Initially, a wintry mix of rain, freezing rain, sleet and snow developed over western and central North Dakota. As temperatures dropped over the northwest and far north central, ice accumulated on power lines. Strong winds developed, with precipitation eventually changing over to all snow. The strong winds combined with the ice on the power lines leading to thousands of power poles and cross arms breaking, resulting in widespread power outages throughout the northwest into the far north central. On the evening of April 24, almost all of the northwest part of the state was without power. Some residential services in rural areas took over three weeks to have power restored, and some non-residential services were not restored until mid-June. As the snow combined with the strong winds, visibility dropped to zero across western and parts of central North Dakota. Interstate 94 closed from the Montana state line to Mandan, and most other roads were impassable. Due to the after-effects of the blizzard and power outages, the American Red Cross opened warming shelters in Crosby and Williston, and provided showers and meals to many individuals. Snowfall totals of over a foot and a half were reported, though were tough to measure with wind gusts over 70 mph. As of the writing of this episode, power companies and government officials were still in the process of calculating financial losses. With that said, expectations are that at a minimum, the financial impact from this storm was at least 60 million dollars. It should be understood that this value, and the individual county event entries, are rough estimates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and heavy snow combined to produce a blizzard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Colorado low lifted slowly through the Northern Plains into the Upper Midwest, resulting in a multi-day blizzard over most of North Dakota. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Colorado low lifted through North Dakota. Initially, a wintry mix of rain, freezing rain, sleet and snow developed over western and central North Dakota. As temperatures dropped over the northwest and far north central, ice accumulated on power lines. Strong winds developed, with precipitation eventually changing over to all snow. The strong winds combined with the ice on the power lines leading to thousands of power poles and cross arms breaking, resulting in widespread power outages throughout the northwest into the far north central. On the evening of April 24, almost all of the northwest part of the state was without power. Some residential services in rural areas took over three weeks to have power restored, and some non-residential services were not restored until mid-June. As the snow combined with the strong winds, visibility dropped to zero across western and parts of central North Dakota. Interstate 94 closed from the Montana state line to Mandan, and most other roads were impassable. Due to the after-effects of the blizzard and power outages, the American Red Cross opened warming shelters in Crosby and Williston, and provided showers and meals to many individuals. Snowfall totals of over a foot and a half were reported, though were tough to measure with wind gusts over 70 mph. As of the writing of this episode, power companies and government officials were still in the process of calculating financial losses. With that said, expectations are that at a minimum, the financial impact from this storm was at least 60 million dollars. It should be understood that this value, and the individual county event entries, are rough estimates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and heavy snow combined to produce a blizzard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed near and east of a deep low pressure system that was situated over south-central North Dakota into the southern James River Valley. Strong surface vorticity occurred with this setup, which led to a brief tornado developing along a weak surface boundary over LaMoure County. The tornado was rated EFU, EF Unknown, by National Weather Service policy as there was no damage to evaluate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog developed during the early morning hours, with visibility under one-quarter mile. Along North Dakota Highway 23, in Mountrail County near the Ward County line, a pickup-truck pulled onto the highway out of a private driveway. A semi-truck pulling a tanker loaded with diesel fuel was traveling along Highway 23 and struck the pickup, resulting in the death of the driver of the pickup. The North Dakota Highway Patrol reported dense fog with near zero visibility at the time of the incident, along with compacted snow and ice on the road surface. A 911 call about the accident came in around 7:55 AM.

With that said, expectations are that at a minimum, the financial impact from this storm was at least 60 million dollars. It should be understood that this value, and the individual county event entries, are rough estimates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and heavy snow combined to produce a blizzard. In addition, periods of freezing rain occurred which accreted on power lines. This, combined with strong winds, led to extensive damage to power lines, cross arms, and poles, and thus widespread power outages.

The strongest wind gusts of 64 mph were reported at the Dickinson and Jamestown airports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A male and female, both 74 years old, from Arcadia, Oklahoma attempted to pass through a low water crossing on the Cannonball River near 96th Ave. SW and 71st St. SW in Hettinger County at around 1:00 PM MDT on May 1. They had crossed successfully at the same location twice the day before. This crossing is locally known to have water running through it during most snowmelt and rain events. A gauge upstream near Regent showed that the river rose approximately two feet on April 30, though still well below flood stage, so no flood warning was in effect. At the time of the attempted crossing, the water was approximately three to four feet deep over the low water crossing. Their vehicle, a 2017 Ford F-250, lost traction and went into the river. The couple placed a call to 911 after they lost control of the vehicle. The vehicle was recovered 1,600 feet to the east of the crossing, with both occupants deceased inside.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A married couple, both 74 years old, died after driving into the water at a low water crossing. This crossing is locally known to have water running through it during snowmelt and rain events. At the time of the attempted crossing, the water was approximately three to four feet deep over the low water crossing.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thin, rope tornado was seen while looking to the north by northwest, from the intersection of 44th st SE and 106th ave SE. Touchdown was brief and lasted less than one minute. Movement was to the northeast. Public photos and videos documented the brief touchdown near Kee Lake, 4 miles south of I-29. Maximum width was 10 yards, with winds up to 65 mph.

The strongest wind gust reported was estimated at 80 mph, which caused tree and roof damage in Foster County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Grafton AWOS reports visibility less than 0.25 miles from 1:35 PM to 5:35 PM, with frequent gusts to 35 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-vehicle crash occurred along Highway 52 one mile east of Sawyer in Ward County in patchy dense fog. The crash involved four commercial vehicles and eight passenger vehicles. Four injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A multi-vehicle crash occurred along Highway 52 one mile east of Sawyer in Ward County. The crash involved four commercial vehicles and eight passenger vehicles. Patchy dense fog was in the area, which significantly reduced visibility and led to the crash. Four injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved through eastern Montana and approached western North Dakota where an unstable environment with elevated deep layer shear was present. One storm became severe, producing half-dollar size hail at Beach in Golden Valley County.

A strong cold front and upper low traversed the area during the afternoon and evening of May 12th. During the afternoon, a larger scale Derecho moved from northeastern Nebraska, through eastern South Dakota and into portions of far southeastern North Dakota and west-central Minnesota. Widespread strong to severe thunderstorms were observed, with large hail, tornadoes, and damaging wind gusts all being reported during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several, large 3 to 5-inch diameter tree branches were broken. Winds were estimated to be 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and upper low traversed the area during the afternoon and evening of May 12th. During the afternoon, a larger scale Derecho moved from northeastern Nebraska, through eastern South Dakota and into portions of far southeastern North Dakota and west-central Minnesota. EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado began at approximately 0710 pm about 2 miles west-southwest of LeMars ND and tracked to the north until 0713 pm when it lifted 3 miles north-northwest of LeMars. Several large Ash and Box Elder tree limbs were broken in shelterbelts. At least 2 wooden power poles were cracked and 2 others were leaning as a result of this storm. Peak winds were estimated at 100 mph, equating to an EF1 rating, with a path length of 3 miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were three tornadoes this day in McLean County, all near Ruso and in less than a one hour period of time. Thunderstorms developed along a variety of weak surface boundaries over southwest and central North Dakota as a weak short-wave trough passed through the area. One small area of storms developed along a very distinct surface boundary near Ruso in McLean County. In less than a one hour period of time three tornadoes developed. Initially, these tornadoes appeared to be of the landspout variety, but then two of the longer-lived tornadoes appeared to become supercellular as noted by rotation aloft which was shown during radar analysis. The first tornado was short lived and caused no damage and so was rated EF Unknown by NWS policy. The second tornado snapped hardwood trees and destroyed an animal shelter, and was rated EF-1 based on that damage. The third tornado developed while the second one was still on the ground, with both remaining on the ground for five minutes before the second tornado dissipated. The third tornado destroyed three small wooden granaries, flipped a large seed cart attached to an air seeder, tossed hay bales, flipped anhydrous ammonia tanks, and tossed hay bales, and was rated EF-2 based on the damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was the third of three tornadoes this day in McLean County, all near Ruso and in less than a one hour period of time. The tornado formed in an open field and approached a farmstead, passing just to the west and south of it. Near the farmstead the tornado destroyed three small wooden granaries, flipped a large seed cart attached to an air seeder, flipped anhydrous ammonia tanks, and tossed hay bales. The tornado continued on south of the farmstead where it tossed multiple hay bales before dissipating. Based on the damage done the tornado was rated EF-2, with winds estimated to be approximately 120 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe weather developed over central into parts of western North Dakota in an environment of enhanced instability and marginal deep layer shear. Many crops were damaged as hail combined with strong wind gusts. The strongest reported wind gust was 76 mph in Mountrail County, while the largest hail was one inch in diameter and occurred with several of the storms in Oliver, McLean, and Mountrail counties. Later in the episode, the focus turned towards flash flooding which occurred in Burleigh County, primarily in and around the city of Bismarck. Vehicles were stranded in flood waters to the south of the North Dakota State Capitol Grounds, and in the 7th Street underpass. Storm sewers were overwhelmed, with manhole covers being pushed off and flood waters escaping.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail combined with winds gusting to over 70 mph to produce extensive crop damage to lentils, wheat, canola, and corn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a frontal boundary over north central into southwest North Dakota. One storm became severe over far north central North Dakota, with one inch diameter hail reported in Rolette County.

Thunderstorms initially developed along and ahead of a weak southeasterly moving cold front over western North Dakota, with a second area of storms developing over south central North Dakota along a staunch outflow boundary. A few of these storms became severe, producing strong wind gusts. The strongest reported wind gust of 61 mph occurred near Watford City in McKenzie County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over Montana ahead of a short-wave trough. The storms formed into a line producing primarily damaging wind gusts, and moved into western and eventually central North Dakota. Intense winds occurred with these storms, especially over the Beach and Fairfield areas where significant damage was reported. In the Beach area of Golden Valley County, estimated 90 mph wind gusts uprooted trees, damaged power lines, and damaged a few structures. Even more damage occurred in the Fairfield area of Billings County where estimated 120 mph winds broke approximately 100 power poles, blew over a two-ton transformer, blew down a communications tower, and damaged or destroyed some structures. A few of the storms produced large hail, the largest being golf ball size, and occurred near Heart Butte Dam in Grant County.

Thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a cold front near the international border, and continued to develop as the front sagged south. Some of these thunderstorms became severe in an environment of enhanced instability and deep layer shear. Damage was reported to crops, structures, and trees with some of the storms. Estimated 90 mph wind gusts to the east of Barton in Pierce County caused tree damage and grain bin damage, while another area of estimated 90 mph wind gusts to the southwest of Orrin in McHenry County caused significant damage to multiple barns. The largest hail reported was the size of a ping pong ball, and occurred in Renville, McHenry, and Pierce counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several barn structures in the area were significantly damaged, with some possibly being a total loss.

An upper level short wave trough moving through the region resulted in severe thunderstorms developing over south central North Dakota. Initially, a severe thunderstorm quickly developed and moved through the Bismarck and Lincoln areas of Burleigh County, with a second storm developing right behind the first one following a similar track. There was a report of tennis ball size hail in the north side of Bismarck, with most locations seeing quarter to golf ball size hail. The area around the Bismarck Airport received no hail. Where hail fell there was widespread roof and vehicle damage. Additional severe storms developed to the south and east, resulting in many reports of crop damage. Although hail was the main threat with these storms, one storm produced reported 65 mph wind gusts near Zeeland in McIntosh County.

A strong upper level short wave trough approached the area, leading to thunderstorms developing over southwest North Dakota. One storm became severe, and produced half-dollar diameter sized hail near Saint Gertrude in Grant County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Plants were damaged around the house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over western North Dakota as an upper level short-wave trough slid through a ridge, combining with a cold front. One area of storms became severe, producing strong wind gusts over portions of Stark and Dunn counties. The strongest wind gust reported was 64 mph northeast of New Hradec in Dunn County.

Thunderstorms developed over Montana ahead of a short-wave trough. The storms formed into a line producing primarily damaging wind gusts, and moved into western and eventually central North Dakota. Intense winds occurred with these storms, especially over the Beach and Fairfield areas where significant damage was reported. In the Beach area of Golden Valley County, estimated 90 mph wind gusts uprooted trees, damaged power lines, and damaged a few structures. Even more damage occurred in the Fairfield area of Billings County where estimated 120 mph winds broke approximately 100 power poles, blew over a two-ton transformer, blew down a communications tower, and damaged or destroyed some structures. A few of the storms produced large hail, the largest being golf ball size, and occurred near Heart Butte Dam in Grant County.

An upper level short wave trough moving through the region resulted in severe thunderstorms developing over south central North Dakota. Initially, a severe thunderstorm quickly developed and moved through the Bismarck and Lincoln areas of Burleigh County, with a second storm developing right behind the first one following a similar track. There was a report of tennis ball size hail in the north side of Bismarck, with most locations seeing quarter to golf ball size hail. The area around the Bismarck Airport received no hail. Where hail fell there was widespread roof and vehicle damage. Additional severe storms developed to the south and east, resulting in many reports of crop damage. Although hail was the main threat with these storms, one storm produced reported 65 mph wind gusts near Zeeland in McIntosh County.

Two areas of severe weather developed during the evening and nighttime hours. The first area was along a cold front over northwest North Dakota, where strong thunderstorm wind gusts were the primary threat, with the strongest wind gust of 69 mph being reported at the Williston Airport in Williams County. The second area was over central North Dakota affecting Wells and Foster counties, where an area of thunderstorms produced strong thunderstorm winds and some large hail. The strongest reported wind gust in this area was 61 mph at the Carrington Airport in Foster County, while one inch diameter hail fell to the west of Hurdsfield in Wells County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were down in Beach, with some trees broken and uprooted. Light poles were blown over at the fairgrounds, with lines down onto campers. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over Montana ahead of a short-wave trough. In the Beach area of Golden Valley County, estimated 90 mph wind gusts uprooted trees, damaged power lines, and damaged a few structures. Even more damage occurred in the Fairfield area of Billings County where estimated 120 mph winds broke approximately 100 power poles, blew over a two-ton transformer, blew down a communications tower, and damaged or destroyed some structures. A few of the storms produced large hail, the largest being golf ball size, and occurred near Heart Butte Dam in Grant County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive damage was reported throughout the community of Fairfield and surrounding areas, especially to the west. Approximately 100 power poles were snapped throughout the area. A pad mounted electrical transformer weighing approximately two tons was blown over about eight miles west of Fairfield. Six miles west of Fairfield, a 120 foot communications tower blew over, and an adjacent small building was demolished. Closer to and in the community of Fairfield, a Quonset hut was blown approximately 150 yards, and a roof on a home was severely damaged. Multiple trees in the area were uprooted and snapped, and multiple stock trailers were flipped over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved from northeast Montana into northwest North Dakota ahead of an upper level low, spreading into north central North Dakota as a low-level jet increased in intensity. During the initial storms, damaging winds were the main threat. The strongest winds were estimated at 95 mph over the Wildrose area in Williams County where outbuildings were damaged, sheet metal detached from buildings damaged vehicles, and trees were uprooted and snapped. Later, storms had a mix of hail and damaging wind gusts. The largest hail reported was three inches in diameter, causing damage in the Williston area of Williams County.

Severe thunderstorms initiated near a surface low and weak frontal zone in northeast Montana. The storms then moved into western North Dakota, spreading south along the front, while the whole complex move towards the southeast. Strong wind gusts were the main threats with these storms, with the strongest reported wind gust of 82 mph occurring in Slope County. One-inch diameter hail occurred in Mountrail and McLean counties.

Strong wind gusts were the main threats with these storms, with the strongest reported wind gust of 82 mph occurring in Slope County. One-inch diameter hail occurred in Mountrail and McLean counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved from northeast Montana into northwest North Dakota ahead of an upper level low, spreading into north central North Dakota as a low-level jet increased in intensity. During the initial storms, damaging winds were the main threat. The strongest winds were estimated at 95 mph over the Wildrose area in Williams County where outbuildings were damaged, sheet metal detached from buildings damaged vehicles, and trees were uprooted and snapped. Later, storms had a mix of hail and damaging wind gusts. The largest hail reported was three inches in diameter, causing damage in the Williston area of Williams County.

An upper level short wave trough moving through the region resulted in severe thunderstorms developing over south central North Dakota. Initially, a severe thunderstorm quickly developed and moved through the Bismarck and Lincoln areas of Burleigh County, with a second storm developing right behind the first one following a similar track. There was a report of tennis ball size hail in the north side of Bismarck, with most locations seeing quarter to golf ball size hail. The area around the Bismarck Airport received no hail. Where hail fell there was widespread roof and vehicle damage. Additional severe storms developed to the south and east, resulting in many reports of crop damage. Although hail was the main threat with these storms, one storm produced reported 65 mph wind gusts near Zeeland in McIntosh County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was the first of two storms that produced severe hail over the Bismarck and Lincoln areas of Burleigh County. Initially, quarter to ping pong ball size hail fell just to the northwest of Bismarck, but hail size increased as the storm moved through the city. Many roofs and vehicles were damaged, along with recreational vehicles at dealerships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed near and ahead of a surface low over southeast Montana, and moved into North Dakota where strong deep layer shear and instability were noted. Some of the storms became severe, with strong wind gusts as the main threat. The strongest  wind gust was 62 mph and occurred in Hettinger and Stark counties. Very heavy rain caused flash flooding in Dickinson, Stark County, which overwhelmed the city's storm water system and affected over 150 homes and businesses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over Montana ahead of a short-wave trough. The storms formed into a line producing primarily damaging wind gusts, and moved into western and eventually central North Dakota. Intense winds occurred with these storms, especially over the Beach and Fairfield areas where significant damage was reported. In the Beach area of Golden Valley County, estimated 90 mph wind gusts uprooted trees, damaged power lines, and damaged a few structures. Even more damage occurred in the Fairfield area of Billings County where estimated 120 mph winds broke approximately 100 power poles, blew over a two-ton transformer, blew down a communications tower, and damaged or destroyed some structures. A few of the storms produced large hail, the largest being golf ball size, and occurred near Heart Butte Dam in Grant County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a cold front near the international border, and continued to develop as the front sagged south. Some of these thunderstorms became severe in an environment of enhanced instability and deep layer shear. Damage was reported to crops, structures, and trees with some of the storms. Estimated 90 mph wind gusts to the east of Barton in Pierce County caused tree damage and grain bin damage, while another area of estimated 90 mph wind gusts to the southwest of Orrin in McHenry County caused significant damage to multiple barns. The largest hail reported was the size of a ping pong ball, and occurred in Renville, McHenry, and Pierce counties.

Thunderstorms developed near and ahead of a surface low over southeast Montana, and moved into North Dakota where strong deep layer shear and instability were noted. Some of the storms became severe, with strong wind gusts as the main threat. The strongest  wind gust was 62 mph and occurred in Hettinger and Stark counties. Very heavy rain caused flash flooding in Dickinson, Stark County, which overwhelmed the city's storm water system and affected over 150 homes and businesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain moved through central Stark County, with over two inches of rain falling in a short period of time over the Dickinson area. Dickinson's storm water system was overwhelmed from the intense heavy rains and caused flooding to basements. Numerous streets were flooded and the underpass along Highway 22 was completely filled. Over 150 homes and businesses were affected by the flooding.

In the Beach area of Golden Valley County, estimated 90 mph wind gusts uprooted trees, damaged power lines, and damaged a few structures. Even more damage occurred in the Fairfield area of Billings County where estimated 120 mph winds broke approximately 100 power poles, blew over a two-ton transformer, blew down a communications tower, and damaged or destroyed some structures. A few of the storms produced large hail, the largest being golf ball size, and occurred near Heart Butte Dam in Grant County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief funnel cloud was observed by Grand Forks airport personnel at a point approximately 12-14 miles to their north northwest. The funnel dissipated about the time of their initial report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Reports of brief funnel clouds were received during the afternoon across portions of eastern North Dakota into the central Red River Valley. These funnels dissipated before reaching the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of a brief funnel cloud in south central Oakwood Township was relayed by law enforcement. No touchdown was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated supercell developed during the afternoon of July 3rd, producing hail up to 1 inch across portions of the southern Red River Valley and west-central Minnesota. In addition to 1-inch hail, a well-defined wall cloud was identified.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Received a report of nickel to quarter size hail, smaller at first then a few larger ones. A supercell thunderstorm with a well-developed wall cloud tracked through central and eastern Dexter Township, into central Liberty Grove Township.  Episodes of smaller hail occurred across the area, with occasional larger hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Colorado low lifted into the Northern Plains and Upper Midwest. Although the system was not very slow moving, it was a very dynamic system which produced very high snowfall rates of nearly three inches per hour in some locations. Many locations over southwest into central North Dakota received over one foot of snow, with the heaviest snowfall of around two feet falling over south central North Dakota in the Bismarck and Mandan areas. Strong winds combined with the snow over south central North Dakota into much of the James River Valley to produce blizzard conditions. Over Dickey County, warmer air aloft resulted in an extended period of freezing drizzle and freezing rain resulting in significant ice accumulations, especially over the eastern portion of the county. This led to damage to power infrastructure, and power outages to over 150 meters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dickinson received 13 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system moved from southern Saskatchewan through southern Manitoba producing a strong pressure gradient over western and central North Dakota. This led to high winds developing. The strongest reported wind gust was 67 mph near Denhoff in Sheridan County. One direct death and one direct injury occurred in Lamoure County with this high wind episode. Along Highway 281 ten miles north of Edgeley at 9:07 AM CST on November 6, a pickup truck was northbound pulling an empty enclosed trailer. The trailer was flipped by the high winds and detached, causing the pickup truck to rotate left into the southbound lane, into the path of a semi-truck that was attempting to pass the pickup truck. The pickup truck was struck and subsequently overturned. The driver of the pickup truck was seriously injured, and the passenger died.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jamestown ASOS reported wind gusts to 64 mph.

Many locations over southwest into central North Dakota received over one foot of snow, with the heaviest snowfall of around two feet falling over south central North Dakota in the Bismarck and Mandan areas. Strong winds combined with the snow over south central North Dakota into much of the James River Valley to produce blizzard conditions. Over Dickey County, warmer air aloft resulted in an extended period of freezing drizzle and freezing rain resulting in significant ice accumulations, especially over the eastern portion of the county. This led to damage to power infrastructure, and power outages to over 150 meters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Williston received nine inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system moved from southern Saskatchewan through southern Manitoba producing a strong pressure gradient over western and central North Dakota. This led to high winds developing. The strongest reported wind gust was 67 mph near Denhoff in Sheridan County. One direct death and one direct injury occurred in Lamoure County with this high wind episode. Along Highway 281 ten miles north of Edgeley at 9:07 AM CST on November 6, a pickup truck was northbound pulling an empty enclosed trailer. The trailer was flipped by the high winds and detached, causing the pickup truck to rotate left into the southbound lane, into the path of a semi-truck that was attempting to pass the pickup truck. The pickup truck was struck and subsequently overturned. The driver of the pickup truck was seriously injured, and the passenger died.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Harvey AWOS reported sustained winds to 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex upper low developed over the central and southern Rocky Mountain Region in the days leading up to this event. It strengthened and brought heavy snow, freezing rain, and mixed precipitation to the Northern Plains. Travel was significantly impacted, with roads and highways closed, and multiple reports of slide-offs and collisions. There were 3 periods of impactful weather associated with this system, with heavy precipitation on the 12th and 13th, light snow and blowing snow on the 14th, then additional light to moderate snowfall on the 15th and 16th. Blizzard conditions were observed on the 16th across portions of central and eastern North Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoOp observer in Michigan, ND reported a measurement of 9 inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Received a public report of an estimated 12.5 inches of snow 3 NW of Barrie. Relayed via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex upper low developed over the central and southern Rocky Mountain Region in the days leading up to this event. It strengthened and brought heavy snow, freezing rain, and mixed precipitation to the Northern Plains. Travel was significantly impacted, with roads and highways closed, and multiple reports of slide-offs and collisions. There were 3 periods of impactful weather associated with this system, with heavy precipitation on the 12th and 13th, light snow and blowing snow on the 14th, then additional light to moderate snowfall on the 15th and 16th. Blizzard conditions were observed on the 16th across portions of central and eastern North Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Received a public report of an estimated 11 inches of snow in Mayville. Relayed via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed along and near a warm front draped through central North Dakota during the afternoon and early evening hours, with many becoming severe. Early in the event, a tornado formed over south central Wells County, north of Chaseley. The tornado impacted no structures and so there was no damage to evaluate, so was rated EF Unknown by NWS policy. Heavy rains over northeast Wells County led to flash flooding, with water flowing over County Highway 5. Elsewhere, large hail was the primary threat, though there were also some occurrences of severe wind gusts. The largest hail reported was the size of a golf ball, which occurred in Stutsman County. The highest wind gust was measured at 70 mph in Kidder County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours across portions of eastern North Dakota and west-central Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Received a public report of hail to at least the size of quarters, with some larger.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms formed in eastern North Dakota during the early afternoon, then moved into west-central Minnesota during the mid to late afternoon. Hail and thunderstorm wind damage were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 4 inches of rain fell in less than three hours over several sections of farm and ranchland in northern Aliceton and southern Big Bend Townships. This primarily impacted the confluence of dead colt creek and the Sheyenne River. Several sections of farmland and farm access roads were flooded and ditches were filled to overflowing upstream of the confluence of Dead Colt Creek and the Sheyenne River. This is near the Dead Colt Creek Reservoir.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms formed during the late evening in eastern North Dakota, then expanded in coverage through the overnight hours. Portions of far northwestern Minnesota were impacted by this same system after midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large, 6 to 10 inch diameter tree branches were broken down, barn window panes blown in, and a couple of power poles tilted across northcentral Galesburg Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm formed in Barnes county during the evening hours, then moved east into Cass County. This storm primarily produced large hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large, 3 to 5 inch tree branches were broken down around a farmstead. Three sections of stock fencing were blown down. Time estimated based on radar.

Low pressure moved through southern Saskatchewan and into southern Manitoba and northern North Dakota. The tight pressure gradient with this system led to gusty west to southwest winds developing over western into central North Dakota. The strongest winds were over portions of western and south central North Dakota, with strongest reported wind gust of 69 mph at the Hettinger ASOS in Adams County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Manning NDDOT site reported a wind gust of 59 mph.

Thunderstorms developed over eastern Montana into western North Dakota as a surface trough moved through the area. Although some large hail fell with these storms, strong wind gusts became the main impact. The strongest winds were estimated at 90 mph in and around the city of Martin in Sheridan County, where multiple large trees were uprooted, with one tree falling on a structure and damaging it. The largest reported hail also occurred in Sheridan County, which was two inches in diameter. In addition to the hail and strong winds, heavy rain fell on southeast Wells County, with the Johnson Township area south of Sykeston suffering flash flooding with almost all roads having water over portions of them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense severe thunderstorms moved from South Dakota into Dickey County near Ellendale during the early morning hours of June 14, producing widespread wind damage along with some hail damage over portions of the southern James River Valley.|The strongest winds, estimated at 110 mph, occurred over the Ellendale area in Dickey County, where many hardwood trees were snapped and others were uprooted, some falling onto homes. Many power poles were snapped with lines torn, and homes were damaged. The strong winds continued to the north and east, with extensive tree damage occurring in LaMoure County at Dickey and Adrian, into Stutsman County near Montpelier. In addition to the damaging winds, large hail also occurred, with the largest reported hail of three inches in diameter falling over Forbes, Dickey County, causing damage to vehicles and homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Siding and roofs of homes were damaged, and multiple vehicles were dented.

The strong winds continued to the north and east, with extensive tree damage occurring in LaMoure County at Dickey and Adrian, into Stutsman County near Montpelier. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive damage occurred throughout the city of Ellendale and surrounding areas. In Ellendale, many hardwood trees were snapped and others were uprooted, some falling onto homes. A home had almost all the shingles and much of the sheathing torn off the roof, and the garage door was collapsed inward. Some power poles were broken in town, and several power service lines to homes were torn. South of Ellendale to the South Dakota state line, multiple power poles were snapped. At the Ellendale Cemetery, multiple hardwood and softwood trees were snapped and others were uprooted. In parts of Ellendale, ping pong ball size hail accompanied the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two areas of severe weather developed during the evening hours. The first area was over south central North Dakota into the James River Valley along a surface trough which moved east. The second area started over southwest North Dakota and moved east along a frontal boundary. The strongest wind gust reported was 79 mph near Medina in Stutsman County, and the largest hail reported was the size of a tennis ball, which occurred near Haynes in Adams County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts were estimated by a trained spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two areas of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and early evening hours. The first area was over south central North Dakota into the James River Valley along a slow moving frontal boundary. The second area was over southwest North Dakota ahead of a progressing surface trough. The largest reported hailstone was two inches in diameter, and fell in Dickey County. The strongest reported winds were estimated at 70 mph, which caused tree damage in LaMoure County. In addition, over the Dickinson area copious amounts of small hail fell, along with heavy rain. Although the largest hailstones were the size of a nickel, the hail piled up closing some streets and stranding some cars. The hail also combined with gusty winds to create hail drifts, some a few feet high.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several tree limbs down.

Thunderstorms developed along and in the vicinity of a frontal boundary that lifted into southern North Dakota as a short-wave trough approached the area. Initial severe storms over Bowman County produced one-inch diameter hail, then the main threat shifted towards severe wind gusts. Over LaMoure County, in the Berlin and Grand Rapids areas, significant tree damage was reported with estimated wind gusts of 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two areas of thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and early evening hours. The first area was over south central North Dakota into the James River Valley along a slow moving frontal boundary. The second area was over southwest North Dakota ahead of a progressing surface trough. The largest reported hailstone was two inches in diameter, and fell in Dickey County. The strongest reported winds were estimated at 70 mph, which caused tree damage in LaMoure County. In addition, over the Dickinson area copious amounts of small hail fell, along with heavy rain. Although the largest hailstones were the size of a nickel, the hail piled up closing some streets and stranding some cars. The hail also combined with gusty winds to create hail drifts, some a few feet high.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public estimated thunderstorm wind gusts of 60 mph.

Thunderstorms developed along and in the vicinity of a frontal boundary that lifted into southern North Dakota as a short-wave trough approached the area. Initial severe storms over Bowman County produced one-inch diameter hail, then the main threat shifted towards severe wind gusts. Over LaMoure County, in the Berlin and Grand Rapids areas, significant tree damage was reported with estimated wind gusts of 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm wind gusts were estimated by a trained spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Colorado Low slowly lifted towards the Upper Midwest, resulting in a multi-day heavy snow event over the Northern Plains, which included western and central North Dakota. The heaviest snow fell over parts of south-central North Dakota into the James River Valley, with some locations reporting as much as 25 inches of snow. Strong winds then developed on the second day of the episode, which continued into the third day. The winds combined with snow already on the ground, along with occasional falling snow, to produce a blizzard across all of western and central North Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: McHenry received 18 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Colorado Low slowly lifted towards the Upper Midwest, resulting in a multi-day heavy snow event over the Northern Plains, which included western and central North Dakota. The heaviest snow fell over parts of south-central North Dakota into the James River Valley, with some locations reporting as much as 25 inches of snow. Strong winds then developed on the second day of the episode, which continued into the third day. The winds combined with snow already on the ground, along with occasional falling snow, to produce a blizzard across all of western and central North Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds, occasional falling snow, and snow on the ground combined to produce a blizzard.
